title: Relapsed/refractory BPDCN after alloSCT in a 67-year-old male

patient.chief_complaint: New painful nodular skin lesions on the left forearm with systemic symptoms
patient.vital_signs: BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 82/min (regular), Temp 36.8 °C, RR 16/min, SpO₂ 96% on room air.

1.setting: Outpatient/Day Unit (Initial Presentation in a Post-transplant Patient)
1.capabilities: You are evaluating a clinically stable but symptomatic patient in an ambulatory or day-unit setting. You can obtain a focused history and examination, request routine blood tests, and arrange standard non-invasive imaging. Invasive tissue sampling and complex diagnostics usually require further coordination and are not performed at the first contact.

2.setting: Inpatient Ward (Early Hematologic and Dermatologic Workup)
2.capabilities: The patient is now admitted for further evaluation of suspected systemic disease. You can organize invasive diagnostic procedures such as marrow and tissue sampling, detailed histopathology and immunophenotyping, and cross-sectional imaging review. Interdisciplinary consultations are available. Some specialized analyses may still be pending.

3.setting: Inpatient Ward / Post-transplant Relapse Strategy Discussion
3.capabilities: You now have results from specialized tests with longer turnaround times, including molecular profiling, chimerism and mutation status, and a comprehensive treatment history. You are expected to integrate these data with comorbidities and prior transplant course to define prognosis and formulate a definitive treatment and supportive care strategy.

demographics.valid_request_if: Requests for demographics or background information such as age, sex, performance status, comorbidities, social and family history, or anthropometrics.
demographics.content:
Age: 67 years
Sex: Male
Performance status: ECOG 2–3 (fluctuating during treatment)
Comorbidities: Arterial hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 2, tachyarrhythmia (episodes of atrial flutter), and a history of acute renal failure during sepsis, post alloSCT & cGvHD.
Social: Married, lives with his wife, two adult children. Former smoker (20 pack-years). No alcohol or illicit drug use.
Family history: Father died of myocardial infarction; mother alive (89 years) with hypertension.
Height/Weight: 179 cm, 86 kg

history_physical.valid_request_if: Requests for history of present illness, review of systems, physical examination findings, or current medications.
history_physical.content:
January 7, 2024: The patient presented to the clinic with new, painful nodular skin lesions on the left forearm that had developed over the previous two weeks, associated with erythema, tenderness, and occasional pruritus. He reported progressive fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, and mild exertional dyspnea. Additional symptoms included night sweats and an unintentional weight loss of approximately 3 kg over the past two months. During the last week, the patient experienced intermittent headaches, transient blurred vision while reading, and occasional tingling around the right corner of the mouth. He denied any seizures or focal motor deficits.
Physical examination: Multiple violaceous, indurated nodules (2-2.5 cm) were noted on the left forearm, tender to palpation, with additional smaller papular lesions on the anterior chest wall. The skin was otherwise dry, without ulcerations. The conjunctivae appeared pale. No oral mucosal lesions were observed. Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (1-1.5 cm), hepatomegaly, and a palpable spleen were present.
Neurological examination: Mildly slowed saccadic eye movements to the right, hypoesthesia in the right infraorbital region, and subtle unsteadiness when walking heel-to-toe. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; no motor weakness or dysarthria. Lungs were clear on auscultation.
Current medication: Amlodipine 5 mg 1-0-0, bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1-0-0, atorvastatin 20 mg 0-0-1, pantoprazole 40 mg 1-0-0, escitalopram 10 mg 1-0-0, tiotropium inhaler 1-0-0, vitamin D 20,000 IU weekly, belumosudil 200mg 1-0-1, valaciclovir 500mg 1-0-0, co-trimoxazole 480 mg 1-0-0 (Mo/Wed/Fr), apixiban 2.5mg 1-0-1.

cbc_chemistry.valid_request_if: Requests for blood counts, coagulation profile, metabolic panel, or cardiac biomarkers.
cbc_chemistry.content:
CBC: Hb 9.7 g/dL, WBC 2.7 ×10⁹/L, ANC 1.1 ×10⁹/L, Plt 83 ×10⁹/L; MCV normal, MCH normal; differential: 40% neutrophils, 22% monocytes, 35% lymphocytes, 3% nuclear debris.
Coagulation: aPTT 32 sec, INR 1.6.
Chemistry: Na 126 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, BUN 23 mg/dL, AST 76 U/L, ALT 86 U/L, bilirubin 1.3 mg/dL, LDH 289 U/L, uric acid 7.2 mg/dL, Ca²⁺ 2.75 mmol/L, phosphate 3.4 mg/dL, lactate 1.3 mmol/L, CK 89 U/L, Troponin T 0.012 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 235 pg/mL.

abdominal_ultrasound.valid_request_if: Requests for abdominal ultrasound or sonography of liver, spleen, and lymph nodes.
abdominal_ultrasound.content: Abdominal ultrasound (January 9, 2024): Hepatomegaly (18.3 cm) and splenomegaly (16.2 cm) with multiple enlarged retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes, the largest measuring up to 2.6 cm in short axis.

echocardiography.valid_request_if: Requests for transthoracic echocardiography or cardiac ultrasound.
echocardiography.content: Echocardiogram: Small pericardial effusion (0.6 cm), mild (grade I/III) aortic stenosis, otherwise preserved left ventricular function (LVEF 53%).

ct_thorax_abdomen.valid_request_if: Requests for CT of the chest and abdomen or cross-sectional staging imaging.
ct_thorax_abdomen.content: CT scan of the chest and abdomen (January 10, 2024): Confirmed a small pericardial effusion (7 mm) and bilateral pleural effusions. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy was noted, including paratracheal nodes up to 1.9 cm and subcarinal nodes up to 1.6 cm. Retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes were enlarged, the largest measuring 3.2 cm. The spleen measured 17 cm craniocaudal. Multiple thickened, contrast-enhancing cutaneous and subcutaneous lesions were observed, consistent with the described clinical findings.

bone_marrow.valid_request_if: Requests for bone marrow morphology and immunophenotyping findings.
bone_marrow.content:
Morphology: The bone marrow was hypocellular, with markedly reduced trilineage hematopoiesis. Erythropoiesis showed nuclear atypia and was relatively decreased, with the erythroid-to-granulocytic ratio shifted in favor of the granulocytic lineage. Granulopoiesis demonstrated hypogranulated precursors with occasional abnormal mitotic figures. Megakaryopoiesis revealed increased numbers of immature forms with atypical morphology. There was infiltration by blastic cells comprising approximately 6% of total nucleated cells. The infiltrating cells were medium-sized, with irregular nuclei, fine chromatin, and scant cytoplasm.
Flow cytometry: The abnormal population was positive for CD4, TCF4, CD303, CD56, and CD123, and negative for lineage-specific B- and T-cell markers (CD3, CD14, CD19, CD34, MPO).

skin_biopsy.valid_request_if: Requests for dermatopathology or biopsy findings of the cutaneous lesions.
skin_biopsy.content: Dermatopathology (skin biopsy, left forearm): Dense dermal infiltrate of medium-sized, atypical, monomorphic cells with irregular nuclei, fine chromatin, and scant cytoplasm, sparing the epidermis. Immunohistochemistry revealed strong positivity for CD123, CD4, CD56, and TCL1, while markers of myeloid or stem cell lineage (MPO, CD34, CD117) were negative.

cytogenetics.valid_request_if: Requests for conventional cytogenetic or karyotype results.

extended_diagnostics.valid_request_if: Requests for extended infectious workup, cardiac rhythm evaluation, or CNS assessment including CSF and brain imaging.
extended_diagnostics.content:
Infection screen: CRP 8.3 mg/L, PCT <0.1 ng/mL. Blood cultures remained sterile. Serum galactomannan was negative.
Cardiology: ECG revealed atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 104/min, regular narrow QRS complexes, and no acute ST-T changes. QTc measured 462 ms.
Lumbar puncture and CSF analysis: Clear CSF; cell count 4/µL. No atypical or blastic cells were detected by cytospin or flow cytometry. Protein 38 mg/dL, glucose 64 mg/dL, within normal limits.
Brain MRI (January 12, 2024): No evidence of meningeal or parenchymal leukemic infiltration. Several small T2/FLAIR hyperintense foci in the periventricular and subcortical white matter, compatible with chronic microangiopathic changes. No diffusion restriction or abnormal enhancement.

molecular_panel.valid_request_if: Requests for molecular profiling, next-generation sequencing results, or mutation panel.
molecular_panel.content:
Mutations detected in TET2 (VAF 26%), ASXL1 (VAF 33%), and TP53 (VAF 13%).
Negative for FLT3-ITD/TKD, NPM1, IDH1/2, CEBPA, and RUNX1–RUNX1T1.
Additional genes tested and negative: ETV6, EZH2, KRAS, NRAS, and SRSF2.

mrd_status.valid_request_if: Requests for measurable residual disease status, key immunophenotypic markers, or summary of active disease.
mrd_status.content: Active disease. ASXL1, TP53, TET2 mutations. BPDCN cells expressing CD123, CD4, CD56, and TCL1.

treatment_history.valid_request_if: Requests for detailed prior history of BPDCN, prior treatments including tagraxofusp, alloSCT, DLI, chemotherapy, and GvHD course.
treatment_history.content:
2021-12-06 – The patient initially presented with erythematous plaques on the left lower abdomen. A skin biopsy performed by the general practitioner yielded no diagnosis. After local excision by dermatology, again without a definitive result, the lesion recurred. Reference pathology ultimately established the diagnosis of blastic plasmacytoid dendritic cell neoplasm (BPDCN). CT showed lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly. Bone marrow biopsy showed 1% infiltration with BPDCN, and mutational analysis revealed TET2, ASXL1, and TP53 mutations.
2021-11-08: Tagraxofusp was initiated at 12 µg/kg/day for 5 days (cycle 1). On day 3, the patient developed grade 3 capillary leak syndrome (CLS) with hypotension (85/64 mmHg) and hypoalbuminemia (albumin 3.1 g/dL). Therapy was halted, and the patient was transferred to the ICU, where noradrenaline was required for one day, along with intravenous albumin (20 g/100 mL TID) and corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day). CLS resolved after five days. Tagraxofusp was resumed for two additional days, but therapy was again stopped on day 5 due to elevated liver enzymes (AST 352 U/L, ALT 256 U/L). The drug was not reintroduced during cycle 1. Liver enzymes normalized after seven days. Regression of skin lesions was noted after the first cycle, corresponding to a clinical partial response. A donor search was initiated.
2022-02-09: Cycle 2 of Tagraxofusp (12 µg/kg/day; ≈1032 µg/day) was administered. Treatment was paused for two days due to elevated liver enzymes and hypoalbuminemia (3.4 g/dL) consistent with grade 1 CLS but was then resumed and completed without further complications or ICU admission. Skin lesions completely resolved, corresponding to a clinical complete remission (CR).
2022-09-22: Bone marrow evaluation demonstrated morphologic CR with minimal residual disease (0.01% CD4+/CD56+/CD123+/TCL1+) by flow cytometry. CT scan showed regression of lymphadenopathy and a decrease in splenomegaly to 14 cm.
2022-09-23: Cycle 3 of Tagraxofusp was given at 12 µg/kg/day for 5 days (total dose 5160 µg). No CLS occurred. Mild thrombocytopenia (121 ×10⁹/L) was noted after this cycle. A matched unrelated donor (MUD) was identified, and allogeneic transplantation was planned.
2022-10-15: Cycle 4 of Tagraxofusp was administered (12 µg/kg/day × 5 days, total dose 5160 µg) uneventfully, without CLS or cytopenia.
2022-11-02: CT scan showed complete remission. Bone marrow was MRD-negative by flow. TET2, ASXL1, and TP53 mutations were no longer detectable.
2022-12-04: The patient underwent allogeneic HSCT from a matched unrelated donor after conditioning with 8 Gy TBI and cyclophosphamide. Immunosuppression consisted of ATG, MMF, and cyclosporine A. Complications included febrile neutropenia treated initially with piperacillin/tazobactam, later escalated to meropenem; central venous catheter infection (Staphylococcus epidermidis) requiring linezolid and later daptomycin (8 mg/kg/day), during which CK increased from 86 to 243 U/L; mucositis requiring parenteral nutrition, and acute renal failure (creatinine peak 3.2 mg/dL). EBV reactivation (1890 copies) was treated with rituximab 375 mg/m² IV weekly x 3 doses. Engraftment with ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L was achieved on 2022-12-24 with G-CSF support. Thrombocytopenia persisted, and eltrombopag 50 mg/day was started. Recipient chimerism <0.06% documented on the same day.
Between 2022-12 and 2023-01, progressive mixed chimerism developed (0.08% → 2.3%). In 2023-02, bone marrow puncture showed minimal residual BPDCN infiltration (0.07%) with persistent TET2 mutation (VAF 5%), while ASXL1 and TP53 remained negative. Peripheral blood chimerism decreased to 6%.
2023-02-15: First donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI) (1 × 10⁶ CD34⁺/kg), followed by a second DLI on 2023-03-13 (5 × 10⁶ CD34⁺/kg).
2023-04: Full donor chimerism (0.00% recipient) achieved and TET2 mutation cleared. The patient subsequently developed late-onset acute GvHD of the skin affecting 70% BSA (stage 3, overall grade 2). Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day was initiated with partial improvement, followed by extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) and gradual steroid taper.
2023-05:  Chronic GvHD of the skin evolved. Ruxolitinib 10 mg BID was started. HSV stomatitis occurred and was treated with acyclovir 1 g TID for 10 days. Due to worsening bone density on routine CT, prednisolone was tapered to 5 mg/day, and denosumab 60 mg every 6 months was initiated.
2023-06: Switched to belumosudil under a compassionate use program due to lack of response to ruxolitinib, prednisolone, and ECP.
2023-07-19: Cutaneous relapse suspected, with multiple nodules on the chest and back. Skin biopsy confirmed BPDCN relapse. CT scan revealed no lymphadenopathy, only mild hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. CSF analysis: negative for involvement. Bone marrow: no infiltration. Molecular testing: TET2 (VAF 3%), ASXL1 and TP53 negative. A third DLI was withheld due to active cGvHD.
2023-07-25: Chemotherapy (Burkitt protocol) initiated (cyclophosphamide 200 mg/m², vincristine 1.0 mg, doxorubicin 25 mg/m², high-dose methotrexate 3 g/m², with intrathecal prophylaxis). Complications: Pseudomonas aeruginosa sepsis treated with meropenem, suspected Aspergillus pneumonia treated with liposomal amphotericin B 350 mg/day, acute renal failure (creatinine 2.1 mg/dL), and atrial flutter requiring electrical cardioversion. ICU admission for 5 days with low-dose noradrenaline, no mechanical ventilation.
2023-08: Partial remission of skin lesions achieved, but due to severe complications, the patient declined further chemotherapy and entered rehabilitation.
2023-09 : Progressive disease documented with skin and bone marrow relapse (0.4% BPDCN). On 2023-09-29, a third DLI (1 × 10⁷ CD34⁺/kg) was attempted but the fourth dose was withheld due to cGvHD flare.
2023-10: Salvage therapy with Tagraxofusp (total cycle 5) reinitiated at 12 µg/kg/day, but stopped on day 3 due to elevated liver enzymes (AST 365 U/L, ALT 345 U/L) and hypoalbuminemia (3.3 g/dL). The patient received IV albumin and prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day was restarted due to GvHD history. Liver enzymes normalized.
2023-11:  Cycle 6 of Tagraxofusp administered without CLS or hepatotoxicity. Cycle 7 was discontinued due to lack of response, as skin lesions progressed. Bone marrow (November 2023): 1% BPDCN infiltration.
2023-12: The patient received local radiotherapy to skin lesions. Best supportive care was discussed; however, the patient expressed the wish to spend the holidays with his family and to decide on further treatment thereafter.

disease_status.valid_request_if: Requests for a concise statement of the current relapse pattern and organ involvement.
disease_status.content: Relapse of BPDCN after alloSCT with skin and bone marrow involvement. No CNS disease.

special_considerations.valid_request_if: Requests for special considerations influencing treatment decisions, such as prior therapies, comorbidities, infectious history, or patient preferences.
special_considerations.content: Post SCT setting with mixed chimerism and multiple DLI attempts. The patient has a difficult infectious history with recurrent Gram-negative bacteremia and fungal pneumonia, requiring multiple ICU admissions. Continuation of intensive chemotherapy is not feasible. Salvage tagraxofusp re-challenge proved ineffective. Compliance has been overall good, and the patient benefits from strong family support. He now presents to the clinic explicitly wishing to discuss alternate treatment options and does not want to transition to best supportive care at this time. Bone marrow reserve is compromised but currently sufficient to allow consideration of further therapeutic approaches.

round_1.working_diagnosis: Unclear lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly and nodular skin leasions
differentials: Relapse of pre-existing hematological neoplasm (prior diagnosis unclear at this stage)

treatment_plan.action: Arrange prompt biopsy of a representative skin lesion to obtain histopathology and immunophenotyping.
Request a staging workup with cross-sectional imaging review to define the extent of nodal, splenic, and cutaneous involvement.
Perform a bone marrow evaluation to assess for marrow involvement and to characterize any abnormal cell population.
Evaluate neurologic symptoms with targeted assessment and consider CNS-focused diagnostics if clinical concern persists.
Provide supportive care including management of hyponatremia and monitoring of renal function, fluid status, and arrhythmia risk in the context of prior tachyarrhythmia and chronic kidney disease.

key_findings_quotes: new, painful nodular skin lesions on the left forearm that had developed over the previous two weeks
Additional symptoms included night sweats and an unintentional weight loss of approximately 3 kg over the past two months.
Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (1-1.5 cm), hepatomegaly, and a palpable spleen were present.
Neurological examination: Mildly slowed saccadic eye movements to the right, hypoesthesia in the right infraorbital region, and subtle unsteadiness when walking heel-to-toe.
Abdominal ultrasound (January 9, 2024): Hepatomegaly (18.3 cm) and splenomegaly (16.2 cm) with multiple enlarged retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes.
CT scan of the chest and abdomen (January 10, 2024): Mediastinal lymphadenopathy was noted ... Multiple thickened, contrast-enhancing cutaneous and subcutaneous lesions were observed.

round_2.working_diagnosis: Blastic plasmacytoid dendritic cell neoplasm (BPDCN) with cutaneous, nodal, splenic, and bone marrow involvement, no CNS disease
differentials: Myeloid or lymphoid neoplasm with plasmacytoid dendritic cell differentiation

treatment_plan.action: Integrate bone marrow morphology, flow cytometry, and skin biopsy immunophenotype to confirm the diagnosis of BPDCN and define disease extent.
Document absence of CNS involvement based on CSF and brain MRI in order to guide systemic rather than CNS-directed salvage therapy at this time.
Assess overall performance status, organ function, and prior treatment exposures to evaluate feasibility of further disease-directed therapy versus palliative approaches.

Continue careful supportive care including management of cytopenias, arrhythmia surveillance, and monitoring for infectious complications in the setting of prior intensive treatments and chronic GvHD.
Begin planning for non-intensive systemic salvage strategies, recognizing prior toxicities with tagraxofusp and chemotherapy.

key_findings_quotes: There was infiltration by blastic cells comprising approximately 6% of total nucleated cells.
Flow cytometry: The abnormal population was positive for CD4, TCF4, CD303, CD56, and CD123.
Dermatopathology (skin biopsy, left forearm): ... strong positivity for CD123, CD4, CD56, and TCL1.
markers of myeloid or stem cell lineage (MPO, CD34, CD117) were negative.
Lumbar puncture and CSF analysis: Clear CSF; cell count 4/µL. No atypical or blastic cells were detected.
Brain MRI (January 12, 2024): No evidence of meningeal or parenchymal leukemic infiltration.

round_3.working_diagnosis: Relapsed BPDCN after alloSCT with skin, lymph node, hepatosplenic, pericardial, pleural, and bone marrow involvement, harboring TET2, ASXL1, and TP53 mutations
differentials: Relapsed BPDCN post alloSCT with limited bone marrow reserve
Advanced clonal myeloid disease with BPDCN phenotype
Secondary myeloid neoplasm in a heavily pretreated post-transplant patient

treatment_plan.action: Initiate salvage therapy with venetoclax plus azacitidine as a non-intensive systemic regimen tailored to relapsed BPDCN in a post-transplant setting.
Avoid further conventional intensive cytotoxic chemotherapy, which is unlikely to achieve durable remission and associated with unacceptable toxicity in this patient.
Do not plan a second allogeneic stem cell transplantation given current performance status and the low probability of obtaining a second complete remission.
Continue comprehensive infection prophylaxis including an azole antifungal, fluoroquinolone during periods of neutropenia, acyclovir, and co-trimoxazole.
Maintain transfusion support for anemia and thrombocytopenia according to symptoms and thresholds, with attention to prior alloSCT and comorbidities.
Integrate early palliative home care involvement while pursuing disease-modifying therapy, to optimize quality of life, symptom control, and advance care planning.

key_findings_quotes: Final diagnosis: Relapsed/refractory BPDCN post alloSCT
Mutations detected in TET2 (VAF 26%), ASXL1 (VAF 33%), and TP53 (VAF 13%).
Active disease. ASXL1, TP53, TET2 mutations. BPDCN cells expressing CD123, CD4, CD56, and TCL1.
Continuation of intensive chemotherapy is not feasible. Salvage tagraxofusp re-challenge proved ineffective.
He now presents to the clinic explicitly wishing to discuss alternate treatment options and does not want to transition to best supportive care at this time.
